learning_outcomes.description: Analyse and describe research designs in the Psychological literature, and to produce appropriate designs for new research.
Write and orally present critical evaluations of empirical research in Psychology, especially in terms of threats to causal inferences about observed relationships among constructs.
Understand and apply methodological techniques to enhance the sensitivity, validity and interpretability of empirical research studies.
Understand the shared aims of researchers in different areas of psychology.
To apply what you have learned about evaluating evidence to decisions in your own life.

assessments.due_date:
3/08/2026 - 30/10/2026

Students who miss a tutorial and wish to make up the associated participation marks should contact their tutor as soon as possible by email, explaining the reason for their absence and providing supporting documentation where available (e.g., a medical certificate). Students whose absence has been approved will be offered an alternative activity to make up the participation marks for the missed tutorial, subject to the timing of the request and the availability of suitable alternative activities.
assessments.task_description:
Tutorials are an important part of the course that will help you apply the concepts introduced in the lectures, and hone your skills of evaluating research. Therefore, it is important that you attend all tutorials. Knowledge of the lecture contents is essential for the tutorials. 

For participation in the tutorials, most students can expect to achieve 90 - 100% of the full participation marks if they study the lecture contents, read the papers for the tutorials, regularly attend and make an attempt to respond to questions and complete the exercises. Students can miss up to two tutorials without any penalty. If students miss more than two tutorials they will lose participation marks proportionally for each day they missed.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Students will be asked to finish their work at the conclusion of the exam. Hence, we do not expect late submissions.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: In-Class Test with short answer questions on case studies
assessments.due_date:
24/09/2026

The quiz will take place during the lecture time in the specified week.
assessments.task_description:
This in-class test assesses your understanding of the material covered in the lectures and tutorials. The test will consist primarily of short-answer questions relating to the design, results, and interpretation of case studies and examples discussed during the course. No additional reading is required beyond the course materials.

To maintain the integrity of the assessment, students may only use their course materials during the test, including lecture slides, tutorial materials, and their own notes. No other resources or aids are permitted, including generative AI or internet searches. Students may use only one electronic device to complete the test (laptop, tablet, or phone). The test must be completed independently and all answers must represent the student's own work.

To help students prepare, a practice test will be provided prior to the in-class test in the tutorials. The practice test is intended to familiarise students with the format and expectations of the assessment, illustrate the types of questions that may be asked, clarify the scoring, and help students prepare thoroughly for the in-class test.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Unless otherwise specified, the test will be administered using Inspera with a secure exam browser.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: Students will be asked to finish their work at the conclusion of the exam. Hence, we do not expect late submissions.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.assessment_title: Final Exam - Short answer questions on a research paper
assessments.due_date:
End of Semester Exam Period

7/11/2026 - 21/11/2026

The paper for the exam will be provided to students two weeks prior to the date of the exam.
assessments.task_description:
This test will assess your ability to apply the lecture and tutorial content. The test consists of short-answer questions relating to the design, results, and conclusions of a research paper that you will be required to read prior to completing the assessment.

Students may consult all printed course materials while completing the test, including the lecture slides, tutorial workbook and their notes. No other resources are permitted. Students must complete the assignment individually and independently, and all answers must reflect the student's own work.

To help students prepare, a practice test will be provided beforehand. The practice test will contain similar questions on a different research paper, providing an opportunity for students to familiarise themselves with the expectations of the task, practice the required skills and receive feedback, which optimally prepares them for the test.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The exam will be conducted on Inspera, with a secure exam browser and students will be asked to finish their work at the conclusion of the exam period.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

Introduction: Overview of the Course and the Scientific Method

I) Introduction: Overview of the Course and how to do well in PSYC3042.

II) The Scientific Method: Constructs, Operationalisations and Variables

learning_activities.topic:
Constructs and Relationships

Constructs, Variables and Operationalisations. Complete exercises on independent and dependent constructs and relations / operationalisations.

Exercises can be completed on your own electronic device, a PC supplied in the computer room (if your tutorial is held in a computer room), or you can print out the Student's Handbook section for this week and complete it with a pen.

Readings: Read Petty et al. for next Lecture + Tutorials!
learning_activities.topic_description: Constructs and Relationships Constructs, Variables and Operationalisations . Complete exercises on independent and dependent constructs and relations / operationalisations. Exercises can be completed on your own electronic device, a PC supplied in the computer room (if your tutorial is held in a computer room), or you can print out the Student's Handbook section for this week and complete it with a pen. Readings: Read Petty et al. for next Lecture + Tutorials!

learning_activities.topic:
Three Types of Validity

Three types of validity: construct validity, internal validity & external validity

Includes: Spurious Results, Reverse Causation, Simpson’s Paradox (as threats to internal validity).
learning_activities.topic_description: Three Types of Validity Three types of validity: construct validity, internal validity & external validity Includes: Spurious Results, Reverse Causation, Simpson’s Paradox (as threats to internal validity).

learning_activities.topic:
Validity, Reliability and Replicability

Complete exercises on identifying IVs, DVs; examine the operationalisations and variables in a published paper (Petty et al.).
learning_activities.topic_description: Validity, Reliability and Replicability Complete exercises on identifying IVs, DVs; examine the operationalisations and variables in a published paper (Petty et al.).

learning_activities.topic:
Basic Designs, Variables and Internal Validi

Overview of the different designs and their strengths and weaknesses.

Different types of validity, relationships, mediation and moderation.
learning_activities.topic_description: Basic Designs, Variables and Internal Validi Overview of the different designs and their strengths and weaknesses. Different types of validity, relationships, mediation and moderation.

learning_activities.topic:
Research Designs

Research Designs: Identify the variables and design features of hypothetical studies.

Readings: Read Amato for next Lecture and Tutorials
learning_activities.topic_description: Research Designs Research Designs : Identify the variables and design features of hypothetical studies. Readings: Read Amato for next Lecture and Tutorials

learning_activities.topic:
Interactions

Ordinal vs. disordinal interactions and (complex) main effects.

Includes also: Floor and Ceiling Effects, Regression to the Mean

Predictions and Interactions

Derive predictions in case studies; identify designs in case studies and published papers; classify and plot interactions.

Confounds, Counter-Confounds and Sensitivity

Confounds, Counter-Confounds and Sensitivity: Definitions, Floor and Ceiling Effects and Regression to the Mean as Confounds; Prevention and Remedies

Confounds and Sensitivity

Analyse hypothetical research designs; consider possible confounds and other problems and their effects on interpretation.

learning_activities.topic:
Confounds: Classification, Prevention and Remedies

Design-Related Confounds, Prevention and Remedies: Discussion of Person Variable Confounds, Ceiling/Floor Effects and Regression to the Mean. Design-related confounds (PAV IVs, Differential Attrition, History and Maturation Confounds.

Confounds related to specific procedures: Materials and Operations confounds; Observer, Experimenter, and Participant Expectancy effects as confounds.

Analyse potential confounds and interpretational issues in published research (Liebert & Baron); find rival hypotheses in excerpts of published research.
